articles.title: ESOP Design for AU Startups
articles.author: blockid-equity-engine
articles.content:
# ESOP Design for AU Startups

A well-designed ESOP (Employee Share Option Plan) is the single biggest equity lever an Australian startup has for talent. Get it right early — it is painful to fix later.

## Pool sizing

- **Pre-seed:** 10% pool. Enough to grant 2–4 early hires generous options without blowing fully-diluted ownership for founders.
- **Seed:** 10–12.5% pool, typically topped up *pre-money* at the seed round (investors expect this).
- **Series A:** 12.5–15% pool, refreshed at the round.
- **Series B+:** 15–20% in aggregate as the team scales.

Rule of thumb: each round, top up to cover the *next 12–18 months* of hires, plus a 20% buffer for promotions and out-of-band grants.

## Grant sizing per hire (% of fully-diluted)

| Role                         | Pre-seed | Seed     | Series A |
|------------------------------|---------:|---------:|---------:|
| First 5 engineers / sr eng   | 1.0–2.5% | 0.5–1.5% | 0.25–0.75% |
| Head of Eng / VP / Director  | 1.5–4%   | 1.0–2.5% | 0.5–1.5%   |
| Mid-level individual contrib | 0.25–1%  | 0.1–0.5% | 0.05–0.25% |
| Advisor (per advisor)        | 0.25–1%  | 0.1–0.5% | 0.05–0.25% |

Grants are at fair market value strike. Use a board-approved valuation (most AU startups rely on the most recent priced round, or a Section 83A startup-concession valuation method for unlisted companies).

## Scheme structure (AU-specific)

- **ESS (Employee Share Scheme)** under Division 83A ITAA 1997 — the standard for AU startups. Options or shares.
- **Startup tax concession (section 83A-33)** — applies where the company is unlisted, Australian-resident, < 10 years old, aggregated turnover < A$50M, and the scheme meets concession conditions. Recipients defer tax until disposal (up to 15 years).
- **Phantom / SAR schemes** — cash-settled. Avoid unless equity isn't possible (e.g. contractor in a hostile tax regime); they lose the CGT discount.

## Documentation checklist

1. Board-approved ESS rules.
2. Grant letter per recipient (template in BlockID equity engine).
3. ESS Statement to recipient each year a taxing event occurs.
4. ESS Annual Report lodged with the ATO (due 14 July).
5. Section 1100W / ASIC Instrument 2022/1021 reliance noted in board minutes.

## Common mistakes

- Granting options *without* an exercise price = a share grant in disguise, taxable at grant.
- Forgetting the cliff — undermines retention purpose.
- Top-ups *post-money* at the next round — dilutes existing investors and signals weak planning.
- No leaver provisions — bad leavers retain fully-vested upside.

articles.title: Vesting Schedule Best Practices (AU)
articles.author: blockid-equity-engine
articles.content:
# Vesting Schedule Best Practices (AU)

Vesting protects the cap table when someone leaves. For Australian startups, the standard is **4 years total with a 12-month cliff** — but the details below matter as much as the headline.

## Default schedule

- **Total vesting period:** 48 months.
- **Cliff:** 12 months. On the cliff date, 12/48 of the grant vests in one tranche.
- **Cadence after cliff:** monthly (1/48 per month) is the AU/global default; quarterly is acceptable for senior hires.
- **Start date:** date of joining the company, **not** the option grant date.

## When to deviate

- **Founders post-product-market-fit:** 3-year remaining schedule with no cliff (they've already earned their stripes).
- **Late-stage execs:** 4 years with a 6–12 month cliff plus a single-trigger acceleration on Change of Control.
- **Advisors:** 2 years monthly, no cliff. Replace anyone not pulling weight after 12 months.

## Acceleration

- **Single-trigger** (acceleration on Change of Control alone) — common for execs, rare for ICs.
- **Double-trigger** (Change of Control *and* termination without cause within 12 months) — the more buyer-friendly standard; recommended for everyone except founders if you want clean exit comparables.
- Founder shares: full single-trigger on Change of Control is standard.

## Leaver provisions

- **Good leaver:** death, permanent disability, redundancy, retirement at normal retirement age. Keep vested shares; unvested bought back at fair value.
- **Bad leaver:** resignation in first 12 months, summary dismissal for cause, material breach. All shares (vested + unvested) bought back at the lower of issue price and fair value, or A$0.001 for unvested.

## Restraints (AU law)

Restraints attached to vesting must be reasonable. Use cascading restraints (12/9/6/3 months × NSW/AU/state/city) so a court can strike the unreasonable layers under the *Restraints of Trade Act 1976* (NSW) without voiding the whole clause.

## Milestone vesting

Use only for advisors or for one-off project grants. Pure time vesting is auditable; milestone vesting is fight-prone. If you must:

- Define each milestone with binary, objective criteria.
- Allocate a fixed share count per milestone.
- Require board sign-off on "achieved_at" before vesting.

## Anti-patterns

- Backdating vesting start dates — ATO does not like it; treated as a separate taxing event.
- Resetting vesting at a new round — signals dysfunction. Top up with a new grant instead.
- Verbal acceleration promises — not enforceable, will surface in diligence.

articles.title: Founder Equity Split Framework
articles.author: blockid-equity-engine
articles.content:
# Founder Equity Split Framework

Founder equity is a multi-year, near-irreversible decision made at the moment founders know each other least. Use a framework, not a gut feel.

## The default is 50/50 — and that is wrong more often than not

The Noam Wasserman / *Founder's Dilemmas* data shows even splits are *correlated with worse outcomes* because the conversation that produces a 50/50 split usually didn't happen. A real conversation, even one that ends near 50/50, is the point.

## The framework (weight × multiplier)

For each founder, score the following dimensions on 1–10 and apply weights:

| Dimension                  | Weight |
|---------------------------|-------:|
| Idea & vision              | 10%    |
| Domain expertise           | 15%    |
| Time committed to date     | 10%    |
| Forward time commitment    | 20%    |
| Capital contributed (cash) | 10%    |
| Risk taken (left job, etc) | 15%    |
| Relevant network           | 10%    |
| Skills critical to MVP     | 10%    |

Normalise each founder's weighted score so the total = 100%. That is your starting cap table.

## CEO premium

The CEO (the one accountable to investors and the board) typically receives a 5–10% absolute premium over the framework score. The CEO carries fundraising load, hiring, and board reporting — quantifiable workload that justifies the premium.

## Vesting always

Even 50/50 cofounders must vest 4 years / 1-year cliff against each other. This is non-negotiable in AU startup diligence; investors will require it at the seed round, and retro-fitting it after a falling-out is litigation territory.

## When to revisit

Never, except via:

- A new grant from the ESOP pool (transparent, board-approved).
- A clawback under the Vesting Agreement (bad-leaver buyback).

Never "adjust" splits in private. Cap-table changes outside the share register are a giant red flag in due diligence.

## Signal: if you cannot have the conversation

If you can't have a 2-hour conversation with your cofounders today about who deserves what and why, you cannot run a company together. The split conversation is a forcing function for cofounder fit.

articles.title: AU Tax Concessions (ESS / ESIC)
articles.author: blockid-equity-engine
articles.content:
# AU Tax Concessions: ESS Startup Concession & ESIC

Two AU-specific tax regimes shape almost every BlockID startup's equity strategy.

## 1. ESS Startup Tax Concession (Division 83A-33 ITAA 1997)

**The concession.** For ESS interests in qualifying startups, the discount is *not* assessed at grant; CGT rules apply on disposal (12-month CGT discount available).

**Eligibility (all must be true at grant):**

- The Company is unlisted on any approved stock exchange.
- The Company is an Australian resident for tax purposes.
- The Company was incorporated < 10 years ago (including all related entities in the corporate group, aggregated).
- Aggregated turnover for the prior year ≤ **A$50M**.
- Scheme conditions: shares offered at ≥ 85% of market value (for shares) or strike ≥ market value (for options); 75% test (75% of permanent employees who have worked 3 yrs must be entitled to acquire ESS interests under the scheme); 3-year holding rule.

**Implication.** Plan grants to slot under this concession wherever possible. The structure is materially better for the recipient than ordinary ESS treatment.

**Documentation:**

- ESS Statement issued to each recipient with a taxing event in the year (by 14 July).
- ESS Annual Report lodged with the ATO (by 14 August).
- Maintain evidence of eligibility annually (turnover, incorporation date, unlisted status).

## 2. Early Stage Innovation Company (ESIC) — Sections 360-15 to 360-50 ITAA 1997

**The concession (for investors, not the company directly).** Investors in an ESIC may claim:

- A **20% non-refundable carry-forward tax offset** on the value of investment, capped at **A$200,000 per year** (sophisticated investor) or **A$10,000** (retail), and
- A **10-year CGT exemption** on the increase in value of qualifying shares (no CGT on sale if held > 12 months and ≤ 10 years).

This is a *huge* fundraising selling point with AU angel investors.

**Eligibility tests (the company must meet both):**

- **Early stage test:** incorporated within last 3 years (or 6 with restrictions); expenses ≤ A$1M and assessable income ≤ A$200,000 in prior year; not listed.
- **Innovation test:** either the **100-point innovation test** (objective gateway scoring R&D spend, accelerator participation, prior equity raises, IP held, etc.) or the **principles-based test** (genuinely focused on innovation, high-growth potential, scalability, broader-than-local market, competitive advantage).

**Reporting:** company files an ESIC report with the ATO for each investor each year shares are issued.

## Practical advice

1. If you're raising in AU and qualify as an ESIC, **say so in the deck**. It's a 20% sweetener for sophisticated investors.
2. Test ESIC eligibility *before* the round closes; the principles-based test is judgement-heavy, so commission a written advice from a tax specialist if the company is anywhere near the borderline.
3. ESIC + ESS startup concession often apply together — your cap table can be designed so founders, employees and investors all enjoy concessional treatment.

refs.source: Tax and Superannuation Laws Amendment (Tax Incentives for Innovation) Act 2016
